explanation: The LTL property ensures that whenever the pilot is in control (standby), the autopilot's state eventually changes from TRANSITION to STANDBY. The property uses the 'U' (until) operator.  The property captures the requirement.  The alternative of just saying F(state_is_STANDBY & standby) would not be correct as it does not capture the transition from TRANSITION.

The LTL property states that eventually (F) the state becomes STANDBY (state_is_STANDBY) after the pilot is in control (standby) while the state is TRANSITION (state_is_TRANSITION) . This accurately captures the requirement that the autopilot transitions to STANDBY when the pilot is in control after the state is TRANSITION.  Previous LTL properties were not provided so no explanation of why they may not capture the input_natural_language can be provided.

The LTL property states that eventually (F) if the pilot is in control (standby), the autopilot state will change from TRANSITION (state_is_TRANSITION) to STANDBY (state_is_STANDBY). This accurately captures the requirement that the transition to standby happens when the pilot is in control.  Other properties like G(standby -> X state_is_STANDBY) would imply that the state is STANDBY in the next state which may not be the case. Also, F(standby & state_is_STANDBY) is too weak as it doesn't consider TRANSITION.

The LTL property translates the natural language requirement by stating that eventually (F) the state will transition to STANDBY (state_is_STANDBY) if and only if the pilot is in control (standby). The use of the 'U' (until) operator can be used to state the same condition with the exception that we are not explicitly indicating that the state must change.

This captures the requirement that a state change happens when the pilot takes control. This correctly captures the requirement that the autopilot changes state from TRANSITION to STANDBY when the pilot is in control.

The LTL property `G(standby -> (X state_is_STANDBY))` states that globally, if the pilot is in control (standby), then in the next state, the autopilot's state will be STANDBY. This correctly captures the requirement that the autopilot transitions to STANDBY when the pilot is in control. Note, this LTL assumes the state change happens immediately. Other LTL properties might have misinterpreted the prompt requiring the autopilot to simply go to standby instead of changing to standby.
